ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Singapore chilli crab meat with rice. FOODON_03311737 (crab dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singapore chilli crab with rice. A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. The rice component is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Singapore chilli crab meat with rice as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving drawn from a shared portion for 2–3 people is approximately 450 g, comprising crab meat in chilli-tomato sauce over steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Crab with Rice — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo: Chilli Crab (cooked crab in chilli-tomato sauce) and Steamed White Rice
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Chilli Crab Meat with Rice' as a combined dish. Values are derived by combining HPB Foodo data for Chilli Crab (cooked crab in chilli-tomato-egg sauce, approximately 55% of dish weight) and Steamed White Rice (approximately 45% of dish weight). The ratio reflects a typical restaurant serving where crab meat and sauce are served over a bowl of rice. Energy and macros are weighted accordingly. Sodium is elevated due to the chilli sauce base.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Steamed white rice (jasmine-type, cooked); chilli crab sauce component treated as negligible GI contributor
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore chilli crab meat with rice as a composite dish. The GI of 54 is estimated from the dominant glycemic contributor: steamed white rice (jasmine-type, GI approximately 64–68 per University of Sydney GI Database). The crab meat and chilli-tomato sauce contribute protein, fat, and acidity, all of which are known to lower the effective GI of the meal. A meal-level GI reduction of approximately 15–20% is applied per peer-reviewed evidence on protein-fat-acid attenuation of rice GI (Atkinson et al. 2021; Sugiyama et al. 2003). Resulting composite GI estimate: 54 (low). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 78.75) / 100 = 42.5, using 450 g serving with 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from chilli-tomato sauce base
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice component with partial attenuation by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 54 and glycemic load of approximately 42.5 per serving indicate a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion or substituting with lower-GI brown rice to improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from crab meat and egg in sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate cholesterol (~68 mg per 100 g) warrant caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; limiting portion size and frequency of consumption is recommended, while the omega-3 content of crab meat provides a modest cardioprotective benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Crab Meat with Rice is a Singaporean Chinese seafood dish featuring extracted crab meat served in a rich, semi-thick chilli-tomato sauce over steamed white rice. It is a restaurant-style adaptation of Singapore's iconic chilli crab, one of the country's most celebrated national dishes, designed for convenient individual consumption without the shell. The sauce is typically prepared by stir-frying garlic, ginger, and fresh chillies in oil, then adding tomato paste, fermented soybean paste (doubanjiang or similar), egg, and stock to create a glossy, tangy, mildly spicy gravy. The crab meat — often from mud crab or Sri Lankan crab — is folded into the sauce and served over a bowl of steamed jasmine rice. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet, tangy, and moderately spicy, with a silky egg-thickened sauce coating the rice. Nutritionally, the dish provides a meaningful source of protein (~9 g per 100 g) from crab meat, moderate carbohydrate from rice (~17.5 g per 100 g), and is relatively low in fat (~3.8 g per 100 g). Sodium is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to the sauce base, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The dish contains crustacean and egg allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal certification without explicit confirmation from the restaurant. Clinically, the moderate glycemic load and high sodium are the primary nutritional considerations. The dish is commonly served as a shared main course for 2–3 people in Singaporean seafood restaurants and is also available on food delivery platforms such as field survey.

confidence_reason: No single HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for chilli crab meat with rice as a combined dish. Nutrient values are derived by weighted composition of HPB Foodo data for chilli crab and steamed white rice. GI is an analogous estimate based on the rice component with protein-fat-acid attenuation. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are from an approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo) and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate: approximately 55% chilli crab (crab meat in chilli-tomato-egg sauce) and 45% steamed white rice by weight, based on typical restaurant serving ratios. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo data for cooked crab and rice — no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. 6. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The dish is described as 'For 2–3 Pax' in the source listing; per-100g values and serving size reflect an estimated individual portion of 450 g drawn from the shared serving. 8. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish may contain fermented soybean products and the restaurant's halal certification status is not verified. 9. The input food_name '蟹肉拌饭' (crab meat mixed rice) is preserved as a Chinese alias; the canonical English name has been normalised to 'Chilli Crab Meat with Rice'.
